findings.summary: performance_monitor.py is a backwards-compat shim that re-exports from performance.py with no documented sunset plan or active external need
evidence: performance_monitor.py contains only: 'from infrastructure.core.performance import CodeProfiler, ProfilingMetrics, benchmark_llm_query, get_performance_monitor, monitor_performance'

Docstring says 'thin re-export' and 'exists for backwards compatibility' but no callers are identified in the packet (avg_fan_in for infrastructure/core/ is 9.6 and no specific fan-in is called out for this file separately)
The shim blurs the boundary between what is the canonical module (performance.py) and what is the compat alias, making it unclear which path new code should use
findings.suggestion: Either remove the shim and update all callers to import from infrastructure.core.performance directly, or document which external callers require the old path and set a concrete deprecation milestone

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/summary_generator.py and infrastructure/reporting/test_summary_generator.py are near-identical copies sharing the same public API and logic
evidence: Both files define identical functions: load_test_results(), load_infrastructure_results(), discover_active_projects(), generate_summary_report(), generate_markdown_report(), run_test_summary_generation()
Both files share identical module-level docstrings describing the same purpose ('Test summary generator for the full repository test suite')
Both files contain identical implementation bodies including the same fallback file path logic (infrastructure_validation_report.json -> coverage_infra.json -> htmlcov/coverage.json)
The reporting __init__.py does not re-export either module, suggesting both exist as parallel entry points rather than as a facade/implementation split
findings.suggestion: Delete one of the two files (summary_generator.py or test_summary_generator.py) and consolidate all callers on the surviving module; update reporting/__init__.py to export the shared functions if needed by downstream scripts

findings.summary: SystemHealthChecker._check_network() performs live outbound HTTP requests inside what is otherwise a local system-state check, mixing two distinct concerns
evidence: _check_network() calls socket.gethostbyname('google.com') and requests.get('https://httpbin.org/status/200', timeout=5) — external calls inside an infrastructure health checker
All other checks (filesystem, memory, cpu, disk, uptime, dependencies) are local system-state reads with no external side effects
_check_filesystem() also hardcodes 'projects/project/src' and 'projects/project/output' as critical paths, which are stale/fictional paths for a template repo whose actual project is 'code_project'
findings.suggestion: Extract network connectivity into a separate optional check or make it injectable. Fix the hardcoded project path strings to use dynamic project discovery or a configurable list

findings.summary: generate_summary_report() in both summary generator modules does data loading, aggregation, weighted coverage calculation, and report dict assembly in a single 120-line function
evidence: Function body spans roughly lines 134-264 and performs: active project discovery, infrastructure result loading, per-project result loading, total-passed/failed/skipped accumulation, weighted coverage calculation, duration accumulation, overall success determination, and full report dict construction
The weighted coverage calculation (lines 165-181) is a distinct algorithm that would benefit from its own named function
The metadata block (lines 226-242) hardcodes 'ollama_server_available': True as a comment 'Based on successful test execution' — this is a false assumption baked into structure rather than computed
findings.suggestion: Extract _calculate_weighted_coverage(results_list) and _build_metadata(active_projects) as private helpers; replace the hardcoded True for ollama_server_available with a passed-in parameter or computed flag

findings.summary: performance_context() starts a PerformanceMonitor but never calls stop(), so callers cannot get PerformanceMetrics from the yielded monitor without calling stop() themselves — the docstring acknowledges this inconsistency
evidence: Lines 155-175: performance_context yields monitor, then finally block computes duration and logs it, but never calls monitor.stop()
Docstring says 'call monitor.stop() explicitly if you need the final PerformanceMetrics object' — the context manager manages a resource but leaves cleanup responsibility ambiguous

PerformanceMonitor.stop() is the canonical way to obtain PerformanceMetrics; the context manager bypasses it, making two incompatible patterns live in the same codebase (context manager vs explicit start/stop)
findings.suggestion: Either have performance_context call monitor.stop() in the finally block and yield or return the resulting PerformanceMetrics, or remove PerformanceMonitor from performance_context entirely and just track time directly, eliminating the misleading yield of an incomplete monitor

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ contains 15 files (9224 LOC) with two of them being near-identical duplicates, and a mix of pipeline reporting, executive reporting, dashboard generation, error aggregation, and coverage parsing without clear sub-boundary separation
evidence: Directory profile shows 15 files, avg_fan_in 2.7 — low cohesion signal for a module of this size
summary_generator.py and test_summary_generator.py are byte-for-byte duplicates of each other, inflating file count without adding decomposition value
multi_project_reporter.py (61 lines) is purely an orchestration glue function that sequences executive_reporter and dashboard_generator — it adds a file boundary for a 3-line pipeline
dashboard_generator.py has 192 call-chain instances at depth 3, flagged as an indirection hotspot, and is the largest single-file concern in reporting at 3456 LOC
findings.suggestion: Merge the duplicate summary generators; consider splitting reporting into reporting/pipeline/ and reporting/executive/ sub-packages given the 15-file count and mixed concerns; inline multi_project_reporter into reporting/__init__ or the executive_reporter module

findings.summary: logging is split into logging_utils.py, logging_constants.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, and logging_progress.py, but logging_utils.py re-imports all of them and is the sole entry point (117 importers), making the split add files without adding navigability
evidence: logging_utils.py imports from all four sibling modules at module level (lines 24-35 and line 79) and re-exports everything through its own namespace
117 external importers all point at logging_utils.py — none of the four split files have independent importers based on the top_imported list
The split modules are listed as outliers in sibling_behavior because they lack get_logger and Path, confirming they are implementation internals not intended for direct import
The result is a hub that must be opened to understand the full logging API, making the split purely an internal decomposition that imposes unnecessary indirection for readers
findings.suggestion: Either collapse the four logging sub-files back into logging_utils.py (if the split does not aid independent testing), or move the logical split to a logging/ sub-package with a clean __init__.py re-export surface, so the decomposition provides navigability rather than hidden internals

findings.summary: performance_monitor.py is a re-export shim missing `from __future__ import annotations` present in all 136 other infrastructure siblings
evidence: performance_monitor.py has no `from __future__ import annotations` import
Sibling behavior data shows `annotations` present in 137/138 infrastructure files
The file is a thin re-export of performance.py, so the omission is purely a style gap rather than a functional need
findings.suggestion: Add `from __future__ import annotations` as the first import line to match the codebase-wide convention

findings.summary: verify_file_integrity uses broad `except Exception` while sibling verify_cross_references uses narrow `except OSError`, inconsistent within the same module
evidence: verify_file_integrity line 77: `except Exception as e:` catches all errors during hash calculation
verify_cross_references line 125: `except OSError as e:` precisely narrows to I/O errors only
verify_data_consistency line 209: `except Exception as e:` broad catch again
Three public functions in the same module use two different exception scoping strategies with no obvious reason for the difference
findings.suggestion: Narrow the broad `except Exception` blocks in verify_file_integrity and verify_data_consistency to the specific exceptions that can occur (OSError for file I/O, json.JSONDecodeError for JSON parsing, numpy errors for numpy loads), matching the precise style used in verify_cross_references

findings.summary: extract_coverage_percentage catches json.JSONDecodeError with a warning, then immediately catches bare `except Exception` silently with only a debug log, losing error signal
evidence: Line 294: `except json.JSONDecodeError as e: logger.warning(...)` — specific catch with warning
Line 298: `except Exception as e: logger.debug(...)` — immediate second broad catch demoted to debug level
The second catch will silently swallow permission errors, encoding errors, and unexpected IOErrors, emitting only a debug message that is invisible at normal log levels
findings.suggestion: Replace the bare `except Exception` with `except OSError` (the only other plausible I/O failure) and promote it to `logger.warning` to maintain consistent error visibility across both branches

findings.summary: Both summary_generator.py and test_summary_generator.py use bare `except Exception` in load_test_results and load_infrastructure_results, swallowing all non-JSON errors silently
evidence: summary_generator.py load_test_results line 40: `except Exception as e: logger.warning(...)` — catches all exceptions including programming errors (AttributeError, TypeError)
test_summary_generator.py load_test_results line 40: identical pattern
Neither module re-raises or narrows; a mistyped key access or unexpected data structure would silently return {} and produce a zero-filled report with no actionable error
findings.suggestion: Narrow the catch to `(json.JSONDecodeError, OSError, KeyError)` — the concrete failure modes for file-not-openable and malformed JSON. Let unexpected errors (e.g., AttributeError) propagate so they surface as bugs rather than silent data gaps.

findings.summary: HealthCheckAPI wraps SystemHealthChecker with four near-identical delegation methods that add no policy, auth, transformation, or logging
evidence: HealthCheckAPI.get_status() calls self.checker.get_health_status() verbatim — no transformation
HealthCheckAPI.is_healthy() calls self.get_status()['overall_status'] == 'healthy' — a one-liner that adds no value over calling SystemHealthChecker directly
HealthCheckAPI.get_metrics() calls self.get_status() and reshapes output, but this reshape could live in SystemHealthChecker.get_metrics() with no loss of clarity
The wrapper has no callers that require a REST adapter contract and no doc indicating it stabilizes a changing interface
findings.suggestion: Collapse HealthCheckAPI into SystemHealthChecker by adding the get_metrics and is_healthy methods directly to SystemHealthChecker. If HealthCheckAPI is intended as a future REST boundary, document that intent explicitly and add at least one policy difference (e.g., caching, auth check) to justify the indirection.

findings.summary: summary_generator.py and test_summary_generator.py are near-identical modules with the same function signatures and implementations, duplicating the boundary logic
evidence: Both modules define load_test_results(project_name), load_infrastructure_results(), discover_active_projects(), generate_summary_report(), and generate_markdown_report() with identical bodies
load_test_results in both files reads from the same path pattern, applies the same 'project' key extraction, and returns the same dict shape
load_infrastructure_results in both files has an identical three-path fallback chain (validation report, coverage_infra.json, htmlcov/coverage.json)
The duplication forces any fix to be applied twice and creates ambiguity about which module callers should use
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single module (keep test_summary_generator.py as it appears newer; delete summary_generator.py) or extract the shared logic into infrastructure/reporting/results_loader.py with load_test_results and load_infrastructure_results, imported by both entry points

findings.summary: check_test_failures accepts an env_var parameter but then also hard-reads MAX_TEST_FAILURES directly, making the parameter contract misleading
evidence: Function signature: check_test_failures(failed_count, test_suite, repo_root, env_var='MAX_TEST_FAILURES', config_key='max_test_failures')

Line 201: `env_value = os.environ.get(env_var) or os.environ.get('MAX_TEST_FAILURES')` — the fallback hard-codes the default env var name, so a caller passing a custom env_var='MY_VAR' will still fall through to MAX_TEST_FAILURES if MY_VAR is unset
The double-read means the parameter does not cleanly control which environment variable governs the limit; the hard-coded fallback bypasses the caller's intent
findings.suggestion: Remove the hard-coded fallback `or os.environ.get('MAX_TEST_FAILURES')` from line 201. The default parameter value `env_var='MAX_TEST_FAILURES'` already provides the default behavior. If the caller passes a custom env_var and it is unset, the function should fall through to the config_key path, not re-read the default name.

findings.summary: extract_coverage_percentage embeds a retry/sleep loop inside a parse function, coupling I/O timing policy with data extraction logic at the boundary
evidence: Lines 253-304: a three-attempt retry loop with `time.sleep(0.5)` lives inside extract_coverage_percentage, which is semantically a parse/extract function
The polling wait is an orchestration concern (waiting for a file writer to flush) embedded in a data-extraction function, surprising callers who expect a pure parse operation
The function imports `time` specifically for this sleep, and the retry count (3) and delay (0.5s) are magic numbers embedded in business logic
findings.suggestion: Extract the retry/polling logic into the caller (scripts/01_run_tests.py or the reporting orchestrator) or into a separate wait_for_coverage_file(paths, retries, delay) utility. Leave extract_coverage_percentage as a pure function that reads and parses whatever files exist at call time.

findings.summary: ResearchTemplate base class in base.py has no direct tests despite being imported by 3 modules
evidence: base.py defines the sole base class ResearchTemplate with a render() method — the only logic-bearing code in the 22-line file
Grep across tests/ finds zero direct imports of infrastructure.llm.templates.base or ResearchTemplate
The holistic_context testing.critical_untested list names base.py (importers: 3) as untested
test_templates.py imports only SummarizeAbstract, ManuscriptTranslationAbstract, and get_template — never ResearchTemplate directly
The error path (KeyError -> LLMTemplateError) in render() is the only executable branch and has no isolated test
findings.suggestion: Add a test class in test_templates.py that directly instantiates a minimal ResearchTemplate subclass with a known template_str and asserts both the happy path and the KeyError -> LLMTemplateError path

findings.summary: infrastructure/llm/templates/manuscript.py (698 LOC, 3 importers) has no dedicated test file; coverage is only incidental via integration paths
evidence: holistic_context.existing_findings notes 'No direct tests (698 LOC, 3 importers) — covered only via imports from tested modules'
No test file named test_manuscript.py exists under tests/infra_tests/llm/
test_review_generators.py exercises ManuscriptExecutiveSummary only via the full review pipeline (requires_ollama path), not as a unit
The PROMPT_COMPOSER_AVAILABLE branch — try/except fallback logic at line 116-127 — is never independently exercised
REVIEW_MIN_WORDS dict is defined twice (lines 34 and 55) in the same module; the first definition is silently overwritten before any test can observe the first value
findings.suggestion: Add tests/infra_tests/llm/test_manuscript_templates.py that instantiates ManuscriptExecutiveSummary, ManuscriptQualityReview, and ManuscriptMethodologyReview with a stub text string and asserts the rendered output contains the required section headers, without requiring Ollama

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/coverage_parser.py (366 LOC, 1 importer) is flagged as a critical untested module with high blast radius
evidence: holistic_context.existing_findings: 'Untested critical module (366 LOC, 1 importers) — high blast radius'

The module contains five parse strategy functions (_parse_failures_section, _parse_failures_verbose, _parse_failures_short, _parse_failures_timeout, _parse_failures_fallback) and the cascading extract_failed_tests dispatcher
No test file references coverage_parser directly in tests/infra_tests/reporting/; test_test_reporter.py exists but coverage_parser is an internal module not re-exported
extract_coverage_percentage has a 3-attempt retry loop with time.sleep(0.5) — timing-sensitive behaviour with no deterministic test
findings.suggestion: Add tests/infra_tests/reporting/test_coverage_parser.py with real pytest-output strings (no mocks) that exercise each parse strategy and the fallback chain, and separately test extract_coverage_percentage with a real temporary JSON file matching the expected structure

findings.summary: REVIEW_MIN_WORDS dict is defined identically twice in manuscript.py, with a comment block copy-pasted verbatim between them
evidence: Lines 34-40: first definition of REVIEW_MIN_WORDS with full comment block
Lines 51-61: identical second definition with the identical comment block (including the same inline comments on 'improvement_suggestions' and 'translation')
The first definition is silently overwritten at module load time; it has no effect
The comment block above lines 51-61 reads '# Minimum word counts for quality validation' — same text as above lines 34-40, word for word
findings.suggestion: Delete the second definition of REVIEW_MIN_WORDS (lines 51-61) and its duplicated comment block; keep only the first definition

findings.summary: Several single-expression methods in PerformanceMonitor carry multi-line Args/Returns docstrings that restate the function name
evidence: record_operation() (line 118): body is `self.operations_count += 1`; docstring is '"""Record an operation."""' — the docstring adds no information beyond the name
record_cache_hit() (line 122): body is `self.cache_hits += 1`; docstring '"""Record a cache hit."""'
record_cache_miss() (line 126): body is `self.cache_misses += 1`; docstring '"""Record a cache miss."""'
update_memory() (line 130): body is two lines; docstring '"""Update peak memory tracking."""' restates the method name exactly
findings.suggestion: Remove docstrings from these trivial counter-increment methods; the method names are already self-documenting

findings.summary: _check_network in health_check.py makes an unconditional live HTTP request to httpbin.org, embedding a production side-effect in an infrastructure health function
evidence: Line 172: `response = requests.get('https://httpbin.org/status/200', timeout=5)` inside SystemHealthChecker._check_network()
The URL is hardcoded as a third-party service for a unit-level health check
health_check.py existing_findings note '1x Hardcoded URL in source code'
get_health_api() returns a module-level singleton, so _check_network fires at any health status query even during import-time side-effect scenarios
findings.suggestion: Replace the httpbin call with a socket-level TCP probe to a well-known IP (e.g., 1.1.1.1:80) or make the connectivity check target configurable; remove the hardcoded third-party URL

findings.summary: extract_coverage_percentage uses a nested retry loop with dual break statements that obscures the early-exit intent and could be replaced with a simpler found-flag structure
evidence: Lines 254-304: outer loop is `for attempt in range(max_retries)`, inner loop is `for coverage_json_path in coverage_json_paths`
On success, inner loop sets `coverage_found = True` then breaks; outer loop checks `if coverage_found: break` — two break guards for one logical exit
The `time.sleep(0.5)` on `attempt > 0` is an embedded timing dependency inside an otherwise pure parsing function
The function mixes file-polling (retry loop) with JSON parsing in a single 60-line body, violating the single-responsibility visible in sibling parse functions
findings.suggestion: Extract the polling retry logic into a helper (e.g., _wait_for_coverage_file) that returns the path once available, keeping extract_coverage_percentage as a pure JSON parser; eliminate the dual break pattern

findings.summary: health_check.py uses a module-level mutable singleton (_health_api) alongside three thin wrapper functions that add no policy, creating unnecessary indirection
evidence: Line 319: `_health_api: Optional[HealthCheckAPI] = None`
Lines 322-327: get_health_api() creates it via global keyword
Lines 330-342: quick_health_check(), get_health_status(), get_health_metrics() are single-expression pass-throughs to get_health_api().method()

The singleton pattern means repeated calls share state across the module lifetime, but HealthCheckAPI has no stateful fields that justify this — it only delegates to SystemHealthChecker which is instantiated fresh each time
Callers who import get_health_status() get different observable behaviour than callers who instantiate HealthCheckAPI directly, without any documented contract
findings.suggestion: Remove the module-level singleton; have quick_health_check(), get_health_status(), and get_health_metrics() each instantiate HealthCheckAPI directly, or document the singleton purpose if shared state is intentional

findings.summary: performance_context() starts a PerformanceMonitor then logs only elapsed time on exit, leaving the monitor.stop() result unused and the start_time state dangling
evidence: Lines 155-175: performance_context() creates PerformanceMonitor, calls monitor.start(), yields monitor, then logs duration with a separate _start time variable
monitor.stop() is never called inside the context manager — only documented as a caller responsibility in the docstring
Two parallel timers exist: monitor.start_time (set in start()) and _start (set at line 170) — they diverge by microseconds and the context manager reports only _start

The docstring says 'call monitor.stop() explicitly if you need the final PerformanceMetrics object' — but after the context exits, start_time is set and stop() is safe to call, making the interface asymmetric
findings.suggestion: Either call monitor.stop() inside the finally block and yield both monitor and metrics, or remove the PerformanceMonitor from performance_context() and use only the simple _start timer, making the monitor available only via the CodeProfiler path

findings.summary: SystemHealthChecker hard-codes httpbin.org as an implicit runtime dependency
evidence: _check_network() issues a live HTTP GET to https://httpbin.org/get with no configurable alternative
This makes the network check dependent on a third-party service that may be unavailable or blocked
No way to override the probe target via config or environment variable
findings.suggestion: Accept the probe URL as a constructor parameter defaulting to httpbin.org, or use a local socket check to avoid the external dependency
